body.p:
The fn:translate() XPath function has been available since XSLT1.0. With more modern variants, such as fn:replace() which also understand regular expressions, there are more powerful alternatives for most use cases.
UWE test footnote (16/40, callout P): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.
UWE supplement (fn-add) for the footnote index.
However, with translate you can replace multiple characters at once. With replace, you would have to nest the functions, as in this example:
Priscilla WalmsleyXML GurusPriscilla Walmsley also offers a function that can perform multiple replacements functx replace-multi.
Replacing individual characters is best done with fn:translate().

In the index sorting of the book index, the group titles of the index sections were normalized using this function in XSLT1.0 stylesheets. To map, for example, the characters AÄÅaäå (note the Swedish ring-A ring-A) to the group title A, the following translate call would suffice:

The capital A would now be the group title for our characters. Of course, you do not want to map each character individually, but rather specify a global rule for the entire alphabet. For the French lowercase letters, this could look like this, for example:

messagepanel.typeofhazard: Here, the order of the character strings matters. Furthermore, the strings must be of equal length.
messagepanel.howtoavoid: Such character strings are best written into a language-dependent separate XML file that could also be maintained outside of the source code.

body.p: Also, checking whether a word is separated by whitespace within a sentence can be realized with fn:translate. A check will certainly also be needed to verify whether a character string already starts with a whitespace (excluding punctuation marks):
With enough creative imagination, there are no limits to the applications of fn:translate().
